questions.answer: A. flag[j] = true and turn = j
questions.to_find: The correct predicate P in Peterson's algorithm that guarantees mutual exclusion

understand.plain: Peterson's algorithm uses two shared variables: a flag array and a turn variable. Process i sets its flag to show intent to enter, then sets turn to j (politely yielding). It then busy-waits while the condition P is true. We need P to be the condition under which process i should wait — i.e., when j wants to enter AND it is j's turn.

keywords.explain: At most one process can be in its critical section at any given time
keywords.example: If process i is in its critical section, process j cannot enter it simultaneously

keywords.explain: A software-based solution to the critical section problem for two processes, using a flag array and a turn variable
keywords.example: flag[i]=true means process i wants to enter the critical section; turn=j means it is j's turn to go first

keywords.explain: A loop that repeatedly checks a condition without doing useful work, used here to hold a process outside the critical section
keywords.example: while(flag[j] == true && turn == j) do no-op; — keeps looping until j is done or yields

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="560" height="220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="530" height="200" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="280" y="40" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Peterson's Algorithm — Process i</text><rect x="30" y="55" width="230" height="30" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="145" y="75" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">flag[i] = true; turn = j;</text><rect x="30" y="95" width="230" height="30" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="145" y="115" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">while (flag[j]=true AND turn=j) no-op;</text><rect x="30" y="135" width="230" height="30" rx="4" class="ok"/><text x="145" y="155" text-anchor="middle" class="sub" fill="white">Critical Section</text><rect x="30" y="175" width="230" height="25" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="145" y="193" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">flag[i] = false;</text><text x="350" y="80" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Process i waits if:</text><text x="350" y="105" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">j wants in (flag[j]=true)</text><text x="350" y="125" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">AND it is j's turn (turn=j)</text><text x="350" y="155" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Otherwise i proceeds</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Peterson's algorithm flow for process i: set flag[i]=true, set turn=j, busy-wait while flag[j]=true AND turn=j, then enter critical section, then set flag[i]=false.

given.aim: Identify the wait condition P in the while loop of Peterson's algorithm that ensures mutual exclusion

terms.meaning: Boolean variables indicating whether process i or j wishes to enter the critical section
terms.example: flag[i] = true means process i wants to enter
terms.connects: If flag[j] is false, j has no interest in the CS so i can safely enter

terms.meaning: Shared integer indicating whose turn it is to enter the critical section when both want to
terms.example: turn = j means j gets priority when both are contending
terms.connects: If turn = i, it is i's turn regardless of j's flag, so i can proceed

terms.meaning: The condition under which process i should keep waiting (not enter the critical section)
terms.example: P must be true when j is both interested (flag[j]=true) and has priority (turn=j)
terms.connects: P = (flag[j]=true AND turn=j) — i waits only when j both wants to enter AND it is j's turn

given.plan: Process i should wait as long as j has its flag raised (wants to enter) AND turn equals j (j has priority). As soon as either j loses interest (flag[j]=false) or the turn changes to i (turn=i), process i can proceed into the critical section.

steps.title: Understand Peterson's algorithm intent
steps.apply: In Peterson's algorithm, after process i sets flag[i]=true and turn=j, it must busy-wait if and only if process j also wants to enter (flag[j]=true) AND the turn variable favors j (turn=j). This is the only case where allowing i to enter would violate mutual exclusion.
steps.note: Setting turn=j is i's way of being polite — saying 'you go first if you want to'. So i should wait only when j actually wants to go AND it is j's turn.

steps.title: Verify correctness of option A
steps.apply: Option A: P = flag[j]=true AND turn=j. If this is the wait condition, then i enters the CS when either flag[j]=false (j does not want in) OR turn=i (it is i's turn). This correctly implements both mutual exclusion (if j is in CS, flag[j]=true AND turn=j at the moment i checks, so i waits) and progress/bounded waiting (eventually turn will change or j will clear its flag).
steps.note: Option B (turn=i) would make i wait when it is i's own turn — that would cause deadlock. Options C and D use flag[j]=false which means i waits when j is NOT interested — also wrong.

solution.result: The predicate P = flag[j] = true and turn = j (Option A) is the correct wait condition that guarantees mutual exclusion in Peterson's algorithm.

questions.formula_note: Peterson's two-process mutual exclusion algorithm: process i waits while flag[j]=true AND turn=j, ensuring at most one process is in the critical section at any time.

questions.question:
Draw the process state transition diagram of an OS in which (i) each process is in one of the five states: created, ready, running, blocked (i.e., sleep or wait), or terminated, and (ii) only non-preemptive scheduling is used by the OS. Label the transitions appropriately.

How many transitions are possible in total in this diagram (counting each labeled directed edge as one transition)?
questions.to_find: The process state transition diagram with 5 states under non-preemptive scheduling, and the number of valid transitions

understand.plain: A process goes through several states during its lifetime in an OS. With non-preemptive scheduling, the OS cannot forcibly take the CPU away from a running process — the process gives it up voluntarily. We need to identify all valid state transitions in this model. The five states are: Created, Ready, Running, Blocked, and Terminated.

keywords.explain: The CPU is never taken away from a running process by force; the process voluntarily releases the CPU by blocking or terminating
keywords.example: If process P is running, no other process can force P off the CPU — P must block (e.g., on I/O) or finish

keywords.explain: The five lifecycle states: Created (just admitted), Ready (waiting for CPU), Running (executing on CPU), Blocked (waiting for event/I/O), Terminated (finished)
keywords.example: A process doing I/O moves from Running to Blocked; when I/O completes it moves from Blocked to Ready

keywords.explain: A directed edge in the state diagram showing a valid event that causes a process to move from one state to another
keywords.example: Running -> Blocked: process issues an I/O request; Blocked -> Ready: I/O completes

understand.visual_alt: State transition diagram: Created --(admit)--> Ready --(dispatch)--> Running --(exit)--> Terminated; Running --(block/I/O request)--> Blocked --(wakeup/I/O complete)--> Ready. Under non-preemptive scheduling there is no Running->Ready (preemption) transition.

given.aim: Draw and count valid transitions in the 5-state non-preemptive process lifecycle diagram

terms.meaning: Process is admitted into the system and placed in the ready queue
terms.example: OS accepts the process and loads it into memory

terms.meaning: Scheduler picks the process from the ready queue and assigns the CPU to it
terms.example: CPU becomes free and the scheduler selects the next process
terms.connects: Non-preemptive means this is the only way a process gets CPU time

terms.meaning: The running process requests I/O or waits for an event, giving up the CPU voluntarily
terms.example: Process calls read() — it cannot proceed until data arrives, so it blocks
terms.connects: Key characteristic — under non-preemptive scheduling the process itself initiates this

terms.meaning: The event or I/O the process was waiting for has completed, so it returns to the ready queue
terms.example: Disk I/O finishes, process is moved back to ready queue
terms.connects: Process cannot go directly from Blocked to Running — must pass through Ready

terms.example: Process calls exit() or returns from main()
terms.connects: Final transition; no preemption means the only other way to leave Running is via block or exit

given.plan: List all states and enumerate valid directed transitions under non-preemptive scheduling. The key absence compared to preemptive scheduling is the Running->Ready (preemption) arc, which does not exist here.

steps.title: List valid transitions under non-preemptive scheduling
steps.apply:
1. Created -> Ready: process is admitted (accept/admit)
2. Ready -> Running: scheduler dispatches process (dispatch)
3. Running -> Blocked: process voluntarily waits for I/O or event (block/I/O request)
4. Blocked -> Ready: I/O or event completes (wakeup/I/O complete)
5. Running -> Terminated: process finishes (exit)
6. Ready -> Terminated: process is aborted while in ready queue (abort) [optional in some models]

Note: Under non-preemptive scheduling, Running -> Ready (preemption) does NOT exist.
steps.note: Some textbooks include a Created->Terminated (process rejected at admission) and Ready->Terminated (process killed while waiting). The core model has at minimum 5 transitions; with the abort transitions the count can be 6 or 7 depending on the textbook.

steps.title: Identify the key missing transition
steps.apply: In a preemptive system there would be a Running -> Ready transition (the OS forcibly preempts the running process and puts it back in the ready queue). Since non-preemptive scheduling is used, this transition is absent. The process can leave the Running state only by blocking (Running->Blocked) or terminating (Running->Terminated).
steps.note: This is the defining difference between preemptive and non-preemptive state diagrams — the absence of the preemption arc (Running->Ready).

solution.result: The process state transition diagram has 5 states: Created, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated. The valid transitions are: Created->Ready (admit), Ready->Running (dispatch), Running->Blocked (block), Blocked->Ready (wakeup), Running->Terminated (exit). The Running->Ready preemption arc does NOT exist under non-preemptive scheduling. Total core transitions = 5 (or 6 if abort from Ready is included).

questions.formula_note: Non-preemptive process scheduling: the Running->Ready (preemption) transition is absent; a process leaves the Running state only by blocking voluntarily or by terminating.
